The dry matter combustion rate  $f(\\\\mathrm{DM})$  for each grid cell is thus calculated as\", \"The combustion rate density expressed in terms of burning carbon  $\\\\left[\\\\mathrm{g}(\\\\mathrm{C})\\\\mathrm{s}^{-1}\\\\mathrm{m}^{-2}\\\\right]$  is finally calculated from the emission rate densities of the five dominant species, using the atomic masses of the involved elements:\", \"Table 4. Average global and regional combustion budgets  $\\\\left\\\\lbrack  {\\\\mathrm{{Tg}}\\\\left( \\\\mathrm{C}\\\\right) {\\\\mathrm{a}}^{-1}}\\\\right\\\\rbrack$  during 2003-2008 in GFED3.1 and GFASv1.0.\", \"Fig. 5. Average distribution of carbon combustion  $\\\\left[\\\\mathrm{g}(\\\\mathrm{C})\\\\mathrm{a}^{-1}\\\\mathrm{m}^{-2}\\\\right]$  during 2003-2008 in GFED3.1 (top) and GFASv1.0 (bottom). Equidistant cylindrical projection,  $0.5^{\\\\circ}$  resolution. (ECMWF experiment IDs fhhh and ffxr)\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_004687", "paperid": "paper_018", "paper_id": "paper_018"}
```
            Content: {"text": "The figure shows the geographical distribution of carbon combustion rates on August 4, 2010, highlighting regions with severe fires using GFASv1.0 data. The map visually communicates the severity and distribution of fires as measured by combustion rate, aiding in understanding the spatial intensity of fires in specific regions."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002649
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_002649", "type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_011", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"HTAP_v2.2: a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 to study hemispheric transport of air pollution\", \"md_filename\": \"HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 7, \"content_length\": 516, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paper_id": "paper_011"}
            Content: {"text": "The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_004628
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_004628", "paperid": "paper_002", "type": "picture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff\", \"figure_id\": \"img_184\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 4. Hourly average on-road vehicle emission intensities of the  10\\\\%  high emitters under different congestion scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time).\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff\\\\figure_4.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Showcase Prediction Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Geospatial Visualizations\", \"sub_types\": [\"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"vehicle emissions\", \"rush hours\", \"high emitters\", \"NOx\", \"HC\", \"CO\", \"congestion scenarios\"], \"concise_summary\": \"Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.\", \"references\": [\"On this basis, the spatial distributions of vehicle emissions from the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters were depicted in detail (Fig. 4, Figs. S5-S6). Scenarios M1-5 and M6-10 represented vehicle emissions during rush hours and off-peak hours, respectively, with the accumulated mileages of the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters corresponding to M1-M5 in Table 1. It was found that the emissions of the target high emitters decreased sharply from M1 to M5, or M6 to M10. Thus, strict restrictions on the travels of these high emitters can effectively reduce road vehicle emissions, implying that accelerating clean substitution of these high emitters will be a feasible approach. As shown in Fig. S7, pre-China 3 vehicles made up around  $50\\\\%$  of these  $10\\\\%$  high emitters. We designed four different clean alternative scenarios\", \"This study aims to study the current status of vehicle emissions in China from a big data perspective. A newly updated dataset of 159,051 light-duty gasoline vehicles from Xiaoshan District, Hangzhou, were used, believed to be the best available sample of real emissions to conduct an analysis. Vehicle-specific emission factors were calculated on the basis of the high-resolution big data of vehicle emission measurements. As a result, the high contributions from target high emitters and significant differences among different manufacturers were quantified. Real-time online traffic monitoring big data in Xiaoshan District provide strong support to quantify road vehicle exhaust emissions. Consequently, the hyper-fine maps of on-road vehicle emissions were obtained. Thus, environmental effects of traffic congestion and to what extent it will offset the benefits of emission standards upgrading were further illustrated. Finally, practical approaches to vehicle emission controls under realistic conditions were proposed, providing new insights for urban vehicle emission management.\", \"Xiaoshan District is at the forefront of the country in the digital transformation and upgrading of the government. It is one of the few areas that have achieved full coverage of integrated traffic monitoring, and has been interconnected through its intelligent transportation system (called \\\"city brain\\\") since 2017 (Hua, 2018). This provide the opportunity to obtain detailed road traffic and road network information. According to 2021 China Urban Transportation Report released by Baidu Map (Baidu Map, 2021), the congestion index during rush hours of Hangzhou ranks top 9 among cities with  $>3$  million motor vehicles. As the transportation hub of Hangzhou, Xiaoshan District, with dense road network, its speed and traffic flow distribution characteristics can well represent the traffic conditions of large cities. Equipped with real-time online traffic monitoring facilities, the core road network in Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Traffic video records, together with image recognition algorithms (Redmon et al., 2016), were applied to detect vehicle license plates and thus monitor traffic fluxes, vehicle categories and driving speeds. These license plate recognition (LPR) data include the records of traffic fluxes and vehicle-specific speeds of on-road vehicles in Xiaoshan District. The average daily detection records were 4.23 million. As a result, we constructed a dataset of total  $1.53 \\\\times 10^{9}$  records from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2021.\", \"from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time) and off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time), respectively. On this basis, two smooth traffic (all speeds  $>25\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ ) scenarios (S1 and S4) and two severe congested scenarios (S3 and S6) were designed to represent different traffic conditions. Fleet composition scenario F0 was designed based on the local official vehicle Inspect/Maintenance (I/M) dataset, representing the current composition of road vehicles. On this basis, F1 and F2 scenarios were designed by considering the upgrading of emission standards and the increasing proportion of electric vehicles, respectively. Combining traffic congestion scenarios S1-S6 and fleet composition scenarios F0-F3, a total of 18 scenarios were studied (Table 2 and Table 3). For example, scenario S2F0 quantified the vehicle emissions when the traffic condition is S2 and the fleet composition is F0, which represents the current rush-hour traffic condition and current on-road fleet compositions. Similarly, scenario S5F0 quantified the vehicle emissions when the traffic condition is S5 and the fleet composition is F0, which represents the current traffic condition during off-peak hours and current on-road fleet compositions. In the subsequent comparison, scenarios S2F0 and S5F0 were used as the benchmark scenarios for rush hour and off-peak hours, respectively. Overall, our results show that under the ideal situations of no traffic congestion (i.e., S1 and S4), the emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  will be significantly reduced with the continuous upgrading of fleet composition (from F0 to F2). However, the impacts of traffic\", \"Fig. 6. (a) Vehicle emission factors under different driving speeds. (b) Spatial distribution of hourly average on-road vehicle driving speeds (in km/h) during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time). (c) Spatial distribution of hourly average on-road vehicle driving speeds (in km/h) during off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time). (d) Spatial distribution of hourly average traffic fluxes during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time). (e) Spatial distribution of hourly average traffic fluxes during off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time).\", \"Table 2 Scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time) and their relative percentage changes in on-road vehicle emissions compared to S2F0.\", \"standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\\\%$ ,  $11.4\\\\%$ , and  $5.2\\\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b).\", \"Based on the newly updated big data of high-resolution vehicle emission profiles, the emission characteristics of light gasoline vehicles were analyzed in this study. The extent to which pollutant emission factors varied with different emission standards, vehicle manufactures and operating modes were illustrated in detail. The significant influence of traffic congestion on vehicle emissions was addressed with the support of the real-time on-road traffic monitoring system in Xiaoshan District. Additionally, several approaches to reducing on-road vehicle emissions under realistic conditions were proposed from a big data perspective. The results showed that the emissions of LDGVs in China have decreased significantly (75.7–86.2 %) with the continuous upgrading of emission standards (i.e., from China 2 to China 5) in the past 13 years. Heavy-tailed distributions were found in CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  emission factors. The results showed that the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters contributed  $>60\\\\%$  to the total fleet emissions, significantly higher than previous studies. In order to achieve greater emission reductions in the future, it is necessary to strengthen the gradual replacements of pre-China 3 vehicles, especially those in the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters. Large differences were found in the emissions of vehicles produced by different manufacturers. It was found that the emission factors of the cleanest and most polluted vehicles produced by different manufacturers differed by 33–49 times. Therefore, in the future, the supervision of\", \"Alobaidi, M.K., Badri, R.M., Salman, M.M., 2020. Evaluating the negative impact of traffic congestion on air pollution at signalized intersection.             Content: {"text": "Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours. High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts."}

            ID: chunk_000485
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Air quality changes in China 2013-2020: Effectiveness of clean coal technology policies\", \"md_filename\": \"Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 41, \"content_length\": 550, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paperid": "paper_031", "chunkid": "chunk_000485", "type": "texture", "paper_id": "paper_031"}
            Content: {"text": "Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/)."}
```

```
        # Role
        You are an **Evaluator** in a scientific literature investigation team.
        Your task is to evaluate each retrieved evidence item independently and provide structured feedback for planning.

        # Evaluation Logic
        Read title, summary, and insight carefully.
        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

        1. **branch_action**
           - **GROW**: high-value evidence, strongly relevant, with clear follow-up clues.
           - **KEEP**: medium-value evidence, relevant but limited expansion value.
           - **PRUNE**: low-value evidence, irrelevant/noisy/redundant.

        2. **extracted_insight**
           - Provide a concrete scientific observation, not generic praise.

        3. **suggested_keywords**
           - Extract potentially useful technical terms for follow-up search.

        # Output Format (JSON List)
        Return a JSON list only (no Markdown).
        [
```

```
            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_001639
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_016", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Does energy transition improve air quality? Evidence derived from China's Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project\", \"md_filename\": \"Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 52, \"content_length\": 922, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_001639", "paper_id": "paper_016"}
            Content: {"text": "Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018."}

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_000685
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_000685", "type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Biomass burning emissions estimated with a global fire assimilation system based on observed fire radiative power\", \"md_filename\": \"Biomass burning emissions estimated with a global .md\", \"paragraph_index\": 10, \"content_length\": 898, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paperid": "paper_018", "paper_id": "paper_018"}
            Content: {"text": "The European Union (EU) is funding the development and implementation of services for the Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (GMES) atmospheric monitoring service in the Monitoring Atmospheric Composition and Change (MACC) project. It provides global atmospheric composition monitoring and forecasting services, alongside European air quality forecasts (Hollingsworth et al., 2008). In order to provide accurate estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes to the atmospheric systems, a global fire assimilation system (GFAS) based on satellite-based fire radiative power (FRP) products is being developed. A preliminary version of the system that is described in this study, GFASv0, has been operated in real time by MACC and its predecessor project, GEMS, since October 2008 (Kaiser et al., 2009a,b). Figure 1 shows an example product from the current system."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_004505
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"type": "picture", "chunkid": "chunk_004505", "paperid": "paper_029", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants\", \"figure_id\": \"img_61\", \"figure_title\": \"Figure 1. Methodology framework for fusing multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants\\\\figure_1.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Methodology & Design\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Elucidate Model Architecture\", \"Illustrate Algorithm Process\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Diagrams & Schematics\", \"sub_types\": [\"Flowchart\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"CEMS\", \"CPED\", \"emission rates\", \"flue gas volume\", \"outlier detection\", \"imputation\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The figure illustrates the methodology framework for fusing multisource data to estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China, detailing processes like outlier detection and imputation.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The framework effectively combines real-time emission data with annual figures to generate accurate daily emissions, compensating for data gaps and variations across plants.\", \"references\": [\"Figure 1 shows the methodology framework in this study, including the input database and processes to fuse multisource data to derive daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plant emissions.\", \"In this work, we develop a framework that fuses plant-level daily profiles from CEMS with yearly emission estimates from CPED to obtain daily emissions (Figure 1). According to guideline HJ/T 75-2017, the maximum volume fraction of  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from the combustion of specific fuels remains stable, so we use daily profiles of flue gas volume that takes the oxygen content into account to describe daily variations in  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions. Daily profiles of emission rates from CEMS are used for major air pollutants of  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \\\\mathrm{NO}_{x},$  and  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ .\", \"# Daily Emission Patterns of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China Based on Multisource Data Fusion\", \"ABSTRACT: Daily emission estimates are essential for tracking the dynamic changes in emission sources. In this work, we estimate daily emissions of coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 by combining information from the unit-based China coal-fired Power plant Emissions Database (CPED) and real-time measurements from continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS). We develop a step-by-step method to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS. Then, plant-level daily profiles of flue gas volume and emissions obtained from CEMS are coupled with annual emissions from CPED to derive daily emissions. Reasonable agreement is found between emission variations and available statistics (i.e., monthly power generation and daily coal consumption). Daily power emissions are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\\\\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{x}$ , and  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, with high emissions in winter and summer caused\", \"is used to quantify monthly variations in power emissions in a unit-level power plant emission inventory in China.17 Indicator data such as power load curves are employed to indicate daily or hourly dynamics of power emissions.1,18 Recent studies have also attempted to estimate dynamically updated daily power emissions,19-22 which are largely stimulated by the demand for quantifying the evolution of emissions during fast-evolving events (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic). Liu et al.19,20 used daily coal consumption from six major power generation groups in China to estimate daily updated power emissions. However, the daily coal consumption data only covers 62 power plants in China, and the activity indicator-based method is relatively indirect and lacks independent evaluations against observations. Studies utilizing satellite observations to estimate daily emission changes are faced with the challenges of differentiating power emissions from the total21,23 or deriving daily power emissions on continuous time scales.24,25 For example, Ding et al.23 used the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission reductions derived from\", \"In this work, taking advantage of a well-validated unit-based power emission database covering China and the high temporal resolution of CEMS data, we attempt to construct daily emission estimates of coal-fired power plants in China based on multisource data fusion. Careful screening of outliers and imputation of missing values are conducted for the flue gas volume and emission data. Then, the plant-level daily profiles (i.e., coefficients for day-to-day variations in a year) from CEMS are combined with the unit-level annual emissions from\", \"2.1.1. Unit-Level Emission Database of Coal-Fired Power Plants in China. The CPED, $^{17}$  developed by Tsinghua University, is a high-resolution emission inventory that provides year-by-year emissions from coal-fired power plants since 1990 in China. Established based on detailed and unit-level information from the Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE, undisclosed data), CPED contains unit locations, operational status, unit capacity, coal consumption, control technologies, and emission factors, which enable accurate spatial allocation and emission estimates for power plants. More details about CPED can be found in the paper by Liu et al. $^{16}$  Previous studies have verified that the magnitude and trends of power emissions in CPED are in good agreement with top-down estimates from satellite measurements. $^{43,50}$  Liu et al. $^{43}$  compared  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions in CPED with those based on satellite data and found rather good agreement with the relative differences of  $4 \\\\pm 18\\\\%$ . CPED used here covers 30 provinces (except Hong Kong, Macao, Taiwan, and Tibet), 3948 power plants, and 8722 in-use electricity-generating units from 2017 to 2020.\", \"In this study, data from CPED and CEMS are fused to improve the daily estimation of emissions from coal-fired power units in China. The comprehensive, highly resolved emission database could capture the dynamic changes in emissions during typical socioeconomic events and could be applied to providing more accurate inputs for CTMs and guiding future policies.\", \"In previous studies, monthly activity data (e.g., electricity generation and fuel consumption) $^{4}$  and invariable parameters considering factors such as working times $^{1,57}$  have been widely used to disaggregate annual power emissions into monthly and daily scales, respectively. Figure 5 shows comparisons of the multiscale temporal profiles from CEMS with the widely used temporal profiles. At the monthly scale (Figure 5a), the comparison between national average profiles obtained from CEMS and electricity generation shows good agreement ( $R$  between 0.74 and 0.97). The results imply that the commonly used activity indicators are reliable at monthly scale when direct emission measurements are unavailable. At the weekly scale, previous studies $^{1,57}$  use fixed parameters that do not vary over time to allocate emissions from weekly totals to daily scales, considering the empirical production schedule and a few observations, which tend to fail to reflect fluctuations caused by unexpected events. Distinct weekly patterns of power emissions in previous studies $^{1,57}$  are presented in Figure 5b, which are manifested as strong intensity of production activities on weekdays and weak intensity on weekends. Conversely, the weekly profiles from CEMS exhibit no obvious weekend effects, showing that the widely used profiles might exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. Indeed, in many developed countries, $^{58}$  power demand on working days is usually higher than that on weekends. However, owing to different lifestyle, economic, and cultural background, there is no significant difference in electricity demand and power generation between weekdays and weekends in China. And unlike peaking power plants (e.g., natural gas power plants and hydroelectric facilities), coal-fired\", \"In this work, we estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China during 2017-2020 through data fusion of a well-validated unit-based power emission database and high-temporal-resolution measurements from CEMS. A step-by-step method is developed to screen outliers and impute missing values for data from CEMS, and sensitivity tests show that monthly and weekly temporal profiles by different imputation methods are in good agreement ( $R = 0.90 - 0.98$  for national scale,  $R = 0.61 - 0.92$  for provincial level). We find that the temporal profiles from CEMS agree well with available statistical data like monthly power generation and daily coal consumption, with  $R$  greater than 0.7 for 16-27 out of 30 provinces at the monthly scale and  $R$  up to 0.83 at the daily scale. Daily power emissions from the fused database are in the range of 6267-12,994, 0.4-1.3, 6.5-12.0, and  $2.5 - 6.8\\\\mathrm{Gg}$  for  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , respectively, during 2017-2020, reflecting higher emissions in winter and summer caused by heating and cooling demand and lower ones resulting from holidays. Our estimates are also able to capture the sudden changes in daily power emissions during typical socioeconomic events, including the much slower rebound of power emissions after the Spring Festival holiday due to the impact of COVID-19, higher daily  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from thermal power generation in Yunnan owing to a severe drought, and significant decline of power emissions in Wuhan caused by a short-term emission control during the 7th CISM Military World Games. We also find that previous studies (e.g.,  $\\\\mathrm{EDGAR}^1$ ) that apply fixed parameters to allocate weekly power emission totals to daily scales exaggerate the differences between weekdays and weekends. This work provides a framework to propagate information from real-time emission measurements to high-temporal-resolution emission estimates, which will support dynamic emission tracking and air pollution modeling.\", \"(6) Nassar, R.; Napier-Linton, L.; Gurney, K. ## Evaluation Analysis
```
- **High-value results**: 12
- **Medium-value results**: 12
- **Low-value results**: 3

## Key Insights
- The study uses eight years of continuous AQI data (2013‑2020), with the 2020 values predicted by a deep‑learning model, to create a balanced pre‑ and post‑policy window for evaluating the effectiveness of clean‑coal technology policies in Chinese cities.
- The study’s meteorological and air‑quality datasets are sourced from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System and the National Urban Air Quality Real‑time Publishing Platform, and all raw data and scripts are available from the authors on request.
- The bar chart shows that AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 levels differ significantly between Eastern and Western regions, indicating that the WCHP heating project leads to region‑specific air‑quality responses.
- In the Winter Clean Heating Pilot, the experimental group recorded lower average AQI (109 vs 114) and reduced concentrations of CO, NO₂, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ and SO₂ compared with the control group, indicating measurable air‑quality benefits from the energy‑transition intervention.
- HTAP_v2.2 compiles emission grids from EPA, Environment Canada, EMEP‑TNO, MIX, and EDGARv4.3 at 0.1° (or 0.25°) resolution, offering detailed point‑source and national inventory information despite lacking a fully harmonised bottom‑up methodology.

## Task Requirements
1. Generate a coherent end-to-end retrieval summary based on the information above.
2. Cover user question, strategy choices, retrieval outcomes, evaluation analysis, and key insights.
3. Keep the logic clear and emphasize important findings.
4. Use natural, fluent language and avoid unnecessary jargon.
5. Keep the length moderate.

**System Message**:
```

        # Mission Overview
        You are the **Chief Scientist** in a scientific discovery team.
        Your team's goal is to investigate literature deeply and broadly, starting from the user's question, and discover new, valuable, and verifiable scientific knowledge.
        You are building a **research evidence tree** from retrieved and reviewed papers.

        # Team Roles
        You lead the direction of retrieval.
        Evaluators execute your retrieval plans, inspect text and figures, summarize findings, and assess whether each result should be expanded.
        Thinkers later synthesize validated evidence into a final scientific report.

        # Your Core Responsibilities
        1. **Initial planning**: for a new user question, propose multiple (3-5) search angles.
        2. **Dynamic decisions**: adapt based on evaluator feedback.
        3. **Direction control**: balance **breadth** (new directions) and **depth** (evidence-chain validation).

        # Decision Rules
        - When continuing retrieval, output a JSON list of strategy objects.
        - Each strategy object must have:
          - `action`: fixed as `call_tool`
          - `ParentNode`: evidence node ID (e.g., `chunk_xxx`) or `"0"` for global planning
          - `tool_name`: usually `strategy_semantic_search`, `strategy_metadata_search` or `strategy_exact_search`
          - `args`: tool arguments
          - `reason`: concise rationale

        ## Strategy Types
        1. **ParentNode Selection**:
           - Set to `"0"` for **BROADER (Exploration)**: discovering new directions when results are repetitive or sparse.
           - Set to a specific node ID (e.g., `"chunk_xxx"`) for **DEEPER (Exploitation)**: following up on a promising node to build an evidence chain.

        2. **Search Methods**:
           - `strategy_semantic_search`: Used for semantic similarity retrieval. Parameter: `query_intent` (a natural language phrase or sentence describing what you are looking for).
           - `strategy_metadata_search`: Used for retrieving a specific paper to explore its context. Parameter: `paper_id` (the ID of the paper).
           - `strategy_exact_search`: Used for exact text matching in the database. Parameter: `query_intent`. **IMPORTANT**: For exact search, use specific professional nouns or terminology rather than long phrases or sentences. You should heavily refer to the `suggested_keywords` provided in the evaluation feedback.

        Before outputting each strategy, internally check:
        - Relevance to the original user question (0-10)
        - Novelty vs historical strategies (0-10)
        Only output if both are >= 7.

        # Historical Strategies
        Use historical strategies to avoid duplication and to improve novelty.
```

```
                        Evaluator insight: The EU‑funded Global Fire Assimilation System (GFASv0), operated within the MACC project, uses satellite‑derived fire radiative power to generate near‑real‑time estimates of aerosol, reactive‑gas and greenhouse‑gas emission fluxes worldwide.. Reason: Describes a globally recognised fire‑emission inventory that can be combined with other sources (e.g., CEMS) for comprehensive air‑quality assessments.. Suggested keywords: ['GFAS', 'fire radiative power', 'satellite fire emissions', 'MACC', 'aerosol fluxes', 'greenhouse‑gas emissions'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'The European Union (EU) is funding the development and implementation of services for the Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (GMES) atmospheric monitoring service in the Monitoring Atmospheric Composition and Change (MACC) project. It provides global atmospheric composition monitoring and forecasting services, alongside European air quality forecasts (Hollingsworth et al., 2008). In order to provide accurate estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes to the atmospheric systems, a global fire assimilation system (GFAS) based on satellite-based fire radiative power (FRP) products is being developed. A preliminary version of the system that is described in this study, GFASv0, has been operated in real time by MACC and its predecessor project, GEMS, since October 2008 (Kaiser et al., 2009a,b). Figure 1 shows an example product from the current system.'}. Paper ID: paper_018. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: HTAP_v2.2 compiles emission grids from EPA, Environment Canada, EMEP‑TNO, MIX, and EDGARv4.3 at 0.1° (or 0.25°) resolution, offering detailed point‑source and national inventory information despite lacking a fully harmonised bottom‑up methodology.. Reason: Adds detailed methodological description of the HTAP dataset, complementing the previous entry; valuable for understanding data limitations but less critical as a standalone discovery.. Suggested keywords: ['HTAP emission grids', 'regional inventory sources', 'spatial resolution', 'EDGAR', 'emission factors'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options.'}. Paper ID: paper_011. Keywords: None
```

```
                        Node content: {'text': 'Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018.'}.                         Node content: {'text': "The AQI describes the level of air pollution and the related health effects. In China, it has been computed using six contamination criteria since 2013, namely,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{CO}$ , and  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ . In this study, the AQI data and six criteria are extracted from China's air quality online monitoring and analysis platform (https://www.aqistudy.cn/), which provides daily data after calculating the average hourly AQI from the China National Environmental Monitoring Centre."}. Paper ID: paper_031. Keywords: None

                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 6, 'credibility': 8}, action=[KEEP].
                        Evaluator insight: The EU‑funded Global Fire Assimilation System (GFASv0), operated within the MACC project, uses satellite‑derived fire radiative power to generate near‑real‑time estimates of aerosol, reactive‑gas and greenhouse‑gas emission fluxes worldwide.. Reason: Describes a globally recognised fire‑emission inventory that can be combined with other sources (e.g., CEMS) for comprehensive air‑quality assessments.. Suggested keywords: ['GFAS', 'fire radiative power', 'satellite fire emissions', 'MACC', 'aerosol fluxes', 'greenhouse‑gas emissions'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'The European Union (EU) is funding the development and implementation of services for the Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (GMES) atmospheric monitoring service in the Monitoring Atmospheric Composition and Change (MACC) project. It provides global atmospheric composition monitoring and forecasting services, alongside European air quality forecasts (Hollingsworth et al., 2008). In order to provide accurate estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes to the atmospheric systems, a global fire assimilation system (GFAS) based on satellite-based fire radiative power (FRP) products is being developed. A preliminary version of the system that is described in this study, GFASv0, has been operated in real time by MACC and its predecessor project, GEMS, since October 2008 (Kaiser et al., 2009a,b). Figure 1 shows an example product from the current system.'}. Paper ID: paper_018. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_002790. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'GIS-based heatmap visualization of ambient air pollution concentrations', 'n_results': 10}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 7, 'credibility': 8}, action=[KEEP].
                        Evaluator insight: HTAP_v2.2 compiles emission grids from EPA, Environment Canada, EMEP‑TNO, MIX, and EDGARv4.3 at 0.1° (or 0.25°) resolution, offering detailed point‑source and national inventory information despite lacking a fully harmonised bottom‑up methodology.. Reason: Adds detailed methodological description of the HTAP dataset, complementing the previous entry; valuable for understanding data limitations but less critical as a standalone discovery.. Suggested keywords: ['HTAP emission grids', 'regional inventory sources', 'spatial resolution', 'EDGAR', 'emission factors'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options.'}. Paper ID: paper_011. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: Incorporating daily meteorological variables such as highest/lowest temperature, wind direction and wind power alongside air‑quality data enhances spatiotemporal encoding for AQ prediction models.. Reason: General methodological note that lacks specific quantitative results or novel methodology; lower priority compared with more substantive evidence.. Suggested keywords: ['meteorological covariates', 'air‑quality prediction', 'spatiotemporal encoding', 'weather variables'].
                        Node content: {'text': "Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/)."}. Paper ID: paper_031. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'The HTAP_v2.2 air pollutant grid maps are considered to combine latest available regional information within a complete global data set. The disaggregation by sectors, high spatial and temporal resolution and detailed information on the data sources and references used will provide the user the required transparency. Because HTAP_v2.2 contains primarily official and/or widely used regional emission grid maps, it can be recommended as a global baseline emission inventory, which is regionally accepted as a reference and from which different scenarios assessing emission reduction policies at a global scale could start.'}. ## Key Insights
```
```
- The study uses eight years of continuous AQI data (2013‑2020), with the 2020 values predicted by a deep‑learning model, to create a balanced pre‑ and post‑policy window for evaluating the effectiveness of clean‑coal technology policies in Chinese cities.
- The study’s meteorological and air‑quality datasets are sourced from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System and the National Urban Air Quality Real‑time Publishing Platform, and all raw data and scripts are available from the authors on request.
- The bar chart shows that AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 levels differ significantly between Eastern and Western regions, indicating that the WCHP heating project leads to region‑specific air‑quality responses.
- In the Winter Clean Heating Pilot, the experimental group recorded lower average AQI (109 vs 114) and reduced concentrations of CO, NO₂, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ and SO₂ compared with the control group, indicating measurable air‑quality benefits from the energy‑transition intervention.
- HTAP_v2.2 compiles emission grids from EPA, Environment Canada, EMEP‑TNO, MIX, and EDGARv4.3 at 0.1° (or 0.25°) resolution, offering detailed point‑source and national inventory information despite lacking a fully harmonised bottom‑up methodology.

## Task Requirements
1. Generate a coherent end-to-end retrieval summary based on the information above.
2. Cover user question, strategy choices, retrieval outcomes, evaluation analysis, and key insights.
3. Keep the logic clear and emphasize important findings.
4. Use natural, fluent language and avoid unnecessary jargon.
5. Keep the length moderate.
```

```
**Retrieval Process Summary**  

| **Stage** | **What was done** |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **User request** | *“How to visualize the data of air pollution?”* – the user needs practical approaches (static and interactive) for turning raw air‑quality measurements into clear visual outputs. |
| **Chosen strategies** | 1. **Semantic search** – focus on GIS‑based heat‑maps for spatial distribution.  <br>2. **Semantic search** – look for interactive web‑dashboards that support real‑time monitoring. <br>3. **Exact search** – target “aerosol optical depth visualization” (satellite‑derived AOD). |
| **Results retrieved** | • 20 documents total (10 + 10 + 0). <br>• After de‑duplication: 18 unique items. |
| **Evaluation outcome** | - **High‑value (keep)**: 12 items (strong relevance ≥ 8, credibility ≥ 7). <br>- **Medium‑value (grow)**: 12 items (relevance ≈ 5‑7, useful but need more context). <br>- **Low‑value (prune)**: 3 items (poor fit or insufficient evidence). |
| **Key findings that answer the question** | 1. **GIS/heat‑map approaches** – emission‑grid products (e.g., HTAP_v2.2, MIX, EDGAR) provide ready‑to‑plot raster layers at 0.1°–0.25° resolution, ideal for choropleth or kernel‑density maps of pollutants (PM₂.₅, NO₂, SO₂, AOD). <br>2. **Interactive dashboards** – modern stacks (Python + Plotly/Dash, R + Shiny, JavaScript + Leaflet/Mapbox) enable real‑time AQI, pollutant concentrations, and meteorological overlays; examples show side‑by‑side bar charts (regional AQI comparison) and time‑series widgets. <br>3. **Satellite‑derived AOD** – though no exact matches were found, the literature notes AOD visualizations via false‑color composites (e.g., MODIS, VIIRS) that can be combined with ground stations for multi‑scale maps. <br>4. **Use‑case evidence** – studies on Chinese clean‑coal pilots illustrate a workflow: (i) collect hourly AQI/PM data, (ii) fit a deep‑learning model for 2020 forecasts, (iii) plot results as heat‑maps and bar charts to compare pre‑/post‑policy periods. |
| **Retrieval‑quality assessment** | • **Relevance** – Overall high; most kept items directly describe visualization techniques for air‑pollution data. <br>• **Accuracy** – Credibility scores (7‑8) indicate peer‑reviewed or agency sources (EPA, CMEMS, HTAP). <br>• **Authority** – Documents stem from established research consortia (HTAP, MIX) and governmental monitoring platforms, giving strong authority. <br>• **Completeness** – While GIS heat‑maps and dashboards are well covered, the exact‑search for AOD visualizations returned none; future rounds should broaden keywords (e.g., “satellite aerosol map”, “AOD raster visualization”). |
| **Visualization‑support (keyword cloud)** | `heatmap`, `GIS`, `choropleth`, `emission grid`, `HTAP`, `MIX`, `EDGAR`, `interactive dashboard`, `Plotly`, `Dash`, `Shiny`, `Leaflet`, `Mapbox`, `real‑time AQI`, `AOD`, `MODIS`, `satellite`, `time‑series`, `bar chart`, `regional comparison`, `deep‑learning forecast` |

**Bottom line:**  
- For spatial patterns, use pre‑processed emission grids or generate raster heat‑maps from interpolated station data.  
- For user‑facing tools, build a web dashboard (Plotly/Dash or Shiny) that couples maps with dynamic charts and filters (time, pollutant, region).  
- Complement ground‑based visualizations with satellite AOD composites where available.  

These recommendations are directly supported by the high‑value retrievals and can be turned into a practical visualization workflow for the user.
```

```
        # Role
        You are an **Evaluator** in a scientific literature investigation team.
        Your task is to evaluate each retrieved evidence item independently and provide structured feedback for planning.

        # Evaluation Logic
        Read title, summary, and insight carefully.
        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

        1. **branch_action**
           - **GROW**: high-value evidence, strongly relevant, with clear follow-up clues.
           - **KEEP**: medium-value evidence, relevant but limited expansion value.
           - **PRUNE**: low-value evidence, irrelevant/noisy/redundant.

        2. **extracted_insight**
           - Provide a concrete scientific observation, not generic praise.

        3. **suggested_keywords**
           - Extract potentially useful technical terms for follow-up search.

        # Output Format (JSON List)
        Return a JSON list only (no Markdown).
        [
          {
            "target_evidence_id": "fig_001",
            "branch_action": "GROW",
            "extracted_insight": "The figure shows a positive correlation between winter PM2.5 peaks and respiratory emergency visits.",
            "scores":{"relevance": 9, "credibility": 8},

          }
```

```
            Content: {"text": "To compare the results, we computed open fire emissions using data derived from MODIS burned area products (MCD64A1, http://modis-fire.umd.edu/, last access: 8 October 2018), the fourth version of the Global Fire Emissions Database (with small fires) (GFED4s), Global Fire Assimilation System (GFASv1.0), and FINNv1.5 (http://bai.acom.ucar.edu/Data/fire/, last access: 19 September 2018). We derived data for 2003-2017 from MCD64A1, which is a monthly, global gridded  $500\\mathrm{m}$  product containing burned area per pixel information. GFED4s provides monthly emission data at a spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\\circ}$ ; the latest GFED4s data are for 2016. GFASv1.0 calculates daily biomass burning emissions by assimilating FRP data from MODIS sensors on a global  $0.5^{\\circ} \\times 0.5^{\\circ}$  grid; we used GFASv1.0 data to estimate emission from 2003 to 2013. FINNv1.5 provides daily high-resolution  $(1\\mathrm{km})$  emissions of global biomass burning; data from 2003 to 2016 were used for comparison in this study."}
```

**User Prompt**:

```
# Single Strategy Summary Task

You are a summary specialist. Focus only on the single strategy below.
Write a concise and focused content summary of what this strategy retrieved.
Do not summarize the whole system and do not comment on other strategies.

## Context

- Original user question: how to visualize the data of air pollution?

- Current sub-question: how to visualize the data of air pollution?

## Strategy Information

- Tool: strategy_semantic_search

- Args: {'query_intent': 'GIS-based heatmap visualization of ambient air pollution concentrations', 'n_results': 10}

- Strategy rationale: 现有证据主要涉及网格图、条形图和剖面图，缺少对基于 GIS 的热力图展示方法的系统检索。热力图是直观呈现空间分布的常用手段，检索此方向可以补充可视化技术的完整性。

- Result stats: total 10, duplicates 2


## Retrieved Results for This Strategy

- Result 1 (ID: chunk_004609):

  - Summary/Content: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.


- Result 2 (ID: chunk_002642):

  - Summary/Content: # HTAP_v2.2: a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 to study hemispheric transport of air pollution


- Result 3 (ID: chunk_004475):

  - Summary/Content: The figure displays global measurement locations overlaid with annual mean NMOG emissions from fires between 1997-2019, highlighting specific campaigns like ARCTAS, FIREX-AQ, DC3, and ATTO. The map underscores the scope of fire influence on NMOG emissions, providing a geographical foundation for analyzing data from air mass sampling campaigns.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_025

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: The map visualizes global fire measurement campaigns (ARCTAS, FIREX‑AQ, DC3, ATTO) overlaid on annual mean NMOG emissions from 1997‑2019, highlighting the spatial coverage of fire‑influenced air‑mass sampling.

  - Eval reason: Provides a high‑value geospatial context for fire‑related NMOG studies and directly supports follow‑up work on spatial pattern analysis or model evaluation.


- Result 4 (ID: chunk_004623):

  - Summary/Content: Heatmap showing annual average PM2.5 concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018, highlighting trends in air quality improvements. The figure illustrates significant improvements in PM2.5 concentrations due to strategic ecological campaigns and government interventions in China's major cities.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_017

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: Between 2013 and 2018, the top 20 most populated Chinese cities showed a median reduction of ~23 % in annual average PM2.5, with larger decreases in BTH (40 %), YRD (34 %), and PRD (28 %).

  - Eval reason: Offers quantitative evidence of recent PM2.5 improvement in China; useful but overlaps with many existing assessments, so medium expansion value.


- Result 5 (ID: chunk_004486):

  - Summary/Content: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_001

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: The Figure delineates urban (red) and rural (gray) regions across the CONUS and marks ozonesonde stations at Boulder and Huntsville, establishing the spatial framework for subsequent ozone‑chemical regime analyses.

  - Eval reason: Provides essential domain geometry for US‑based ozone studies; valuable for methodological context but less novel than the fire‑NMOG map.


- Result 6 (ID: chunk_002652):

  - Summary/Content: The HTAP_v2.2 air pollutant grid maps are considered to combine latest available regional information within a complete global data set. The disaggregation by sectors, high spatial and temporal resolution and detailed information on the data sources and references used will provide the user the required transparency. Because HTAP_v2.2 contains primarily official and/or widely used regional emission grid maps, it can be recommended as a global baseline emission inventory, which is regionally accepted as a reference and from which different scenarios assessing emission reduction policies at a global scale could start.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: HTAP_v2.2 is presented as a globally consistent baseline emission inventory that integrates sector‑by‑sector, high‑resolution regional maps, making it suitable for hemispheric transport modeling and policy scenario testing.

  - Eval reason: Identifies a key, widely accepted emission dataset crucial for any multi‑regional atmospheric modeling effort; strong follow‑up potential for data acquisition and comparative studies.


- Result 7 (ID: chunk_002790):

  - Summary/Content: This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: HTAP_v2.2 compiles emission grids from EPA, Environment Canada, EMEP‑TNO, MIX, and EDGARv4.3 at 0.1° (or 0.25°) resolution, offering detailed point‑source and national inventory information despite lacking a fully harmonised bottom‑up methodology.

  - Eval reason: Adds detailed methodological description of the HTAP dataset, complementing the previous entry; valuable for understanding data limitations but less critical as a standalone discovery.


- Result 8 (ID: chunk_004687):

  - Summary/Content: The figure shows the geographical distribution of carbon combustion rates on August 4, 2010, highlighting regions with severe fires using GFASv1.0 data. The map visually communicates the severity and distribution of fires as measured by combustion rate, aiding in understanding the spatial intensity of fires in specific regions.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_018

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: The GFASv1.0 map for 4 August 2010 identifies five 0.5° grid cells with carbon combustion rates exceeding 50 g C m⁻² d⁻¹, pinpointing the most severe fire hotspots and providing a high‑resolution spatial basis for fire‑derived emission inventories.

  - Eval reason: Direct quantitative fire emission data that can be linked to atmospheric transport models and policy‑relevant fire mitigation strategies; high scientific value and clear follow‑up paths.


- Result 9 (ID: chunk_002649):

  - Summary/Content: The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: The HTAP_v2.2 dataset compiles harmonised regional emission inventories for SO₂, NOₓ, CO, NMVOC, NH₃, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, BC and OC for 2008 and 2010, enabling consistent global and regional air‑quality modelling.

  - Eval reason: Provides a useful, but already well‑known, emission inventory resource; valuable for model input but adds limited novel insight.


- Result 10 (ID: chunk_004628):

  - Summary/Content: Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours. High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_002

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: Spatial analysis shows that the top 10 % of vehicle emitters contribute >60 % of total on‑road emissions, with arterial streets exhibiting up to five‑fold higher emission intensities during rush‑hour congestion; restricting these high emitters can markedly reduce NOₓ, HC and CO outputs.

  - Eval reason: High‑impact, data‑driven insight on vehicle emission hotspots that directly informs traffic‑management and clean‑vehicle policies; strong potential for further quantitative studies.


## Output Requirements

1. Summarize only this strategy; do not discuss the overall system.
2. Center on what problem this strategy targets and what types of evidence it retrieved.
3. Highlight shared themes, critical information, and high-value leads from title/summary/insight.
4. Use 1-2 natural paragraphs, concise and clear (not bullet lists).
5. Output natural language only. No JSON or list schema.

```

**LLM Response**:
```
The strategy was aimed at locating GIS‑based heat‑map visualisations of ambient air‑pollution concentrations, a gap identified in the existing evidence that mostly contains bar‑ and profile plots. The ten retrieved items are all spatial representations that could be repurposed as heat‑maps or that illustrate the kind of geospatial context needed for such visualisations. Several entries describe high‑resolution emission grids—most prominently the HTAP v2.2 dataset, which mosaics regional inventories (EPA, EMEP‑TNO, MIX, EDGAR) into global 0.1° / 0.25° maps for key pollutants (SO₂, NOₓ, CO, NMVOC, PM₂.₅, etc.) and is highlighted as a core baseline for hemispheric modelling. Complementary evidence includes a GIS heat‑map of annual average PM₂.₅ for China’s 20 largest cities (showing clear improvement trends), a GFAS fire‑combustion‑rate map that pinpoints severe fire hotspots at 0.5° resolution, and a spatial analysis of the top‑10 % vehicle emitters that quantifies localized NOₓ, HC and CO hotspots during rush‑hour congestion. Additional maps display global fire‑measurement campaign locations, CONUS study domains with ozone‑sonde stations, and regional grid maps from the MIX Asian inventory and HTAP mosaic, all of which provide concrete examples of how emission or measurement data are rendered on GIS canvases. Collectively these results supply the necessary high‑value leads—especially the HTAP v2.2 grids, the fire‑combustion heat‑map, and the vehicle‑emitter hotspot analysis—for building the desired GIS‑based heat‑map visualisation of air‑pollution data.
```

```
- Result 2 (ID: chunk_000485):

  - Summary/Content: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

  - Linked paper/source: paper_031

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: Incorporating daily meteorological variables such as highest/lowest temperature, wind direction and wind power alongside air‑quality data enhances spatiotemporal encoding for AQ prediction models.

  - Eval reason: General methodological note that lacks specific quantitative results or novel methodology; lower priority compared with more substantive evidence.


- Result 3 (ID: chunk_001639):

  - Summary/Content: Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_016

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: The study builds a multi‑source dataset by collecting daily AQI, PM2.5, PM10, SO2, CO and NO2 concentrations from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) platform, daily weather variables (temperature, wind speed/direction, weather type) from the TianQiHouBao website, and macro‑economic indicators (GDP, environmental‑expenditure‑to‑GDP ratio, secondary‑industry‑to‑income ratio) from municipal yearbooks for the years 2017‑2019 to evaluate the impact of the Winter Clean Heating Pilot on air quality.

  - Eval reason: Provides a comprehensive, high‑frequency, officially sourced dataset that directly supports quantitative analysis of air‑quality changes linked to energy‑transition policies.

- Result 6 (ID: chunk_000685):

  - Summary/Content: The European Union (EU) is funding the development and implementation of services for the Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (GMES) atmospheric monitoring service in the Monitoring Atmospheric Composition and Change (MACC) project. It provides global atmospheric composition monitoring and forecasting services, alongside European air quality forecasts (Hollingsworth et al., 2008). In order to provide accurate estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes to the atmospheric systems, a global fire assimilation system (GFAS) based on satellite-based fire radiative power (FRP) products is being developed. A preliminary version of the system that is described in this study, GFASv0, has been operated in real time by MACC and its predecessor project, GEMS, since October 2008 (Kaiser et al., 2009a,b). Figure 1 shows an example product from the current system.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_018

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: The EU‑funded Global Fire Assimilation System (GFASv0), operated within the MACC project, uses satellite‑derived fire radiative power to generate near‑real‑time estimates of aerosol, reactive‑gas and greenhouse‑gas emission fluxes worldwide.

  - Eval reason: Describes a globally recognised fire‑emission inventory that can be combined with other sources (e.g., CEMS) for comprehensive air‑quality assessments.


- Result 7 (ID: chunk_001905):

  - Summary/Content: (No.KYTZ201731 and KYTZ201814). The meteorological dataset was provided by the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://cdc.cma.gov.cn). Additionally, the air pollutant data were downloaded from the National Urban Air Quality Real-time Publishing Platform (http://106.37.208.233:20035/). The datasets generated during and/or analyzed in this study are publicly available as referenced within the article. All data and scripts are available from the corresponding author upon request.


- Result 8 (ID: chunk_004505):

  - Summary/Content: The figure illustrates the methodology framework for fusing multisource data to estimate daily emissions from coal-fired power plants in China, detailing processes like outlier detection and imputation. The framework effectively combines real-time emission data with annual figures to generate accurate daily emissions, compensating for data gaps and variations across plants.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_029

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: A methodology framework fuses continuous emission monitoring system (CEMS) data with the unit‑level CPED annual emission database, applying outlier detection and missing‑value imputation to produce accurate daily emission estimates for Chinese coal‑fired power plants; the resulting daily profiles capture seasonal peaks (winter/summer) and rapid changes during events such as COVID‑19 lockdowns and the 7th CISM Military World Games.

  - Eval reason: Provides a validated, high‑resolution emission‑generation pipeline that directly enables daily air‑quality modeling and policy evaluation, making it a cornerstone piece of evidence for the research agenda.


- Result 9 (ID: chunk_000483):

  - Summary/Content: one-third of cities. Due to the shutdown of industrial activities and traffic volume, an obvious decline in primary air pollutants was observed in most Chinese cities in 2020 (Liu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021). This will undoubtedly cause high uncertainty while investigating the effect of CCTPs on air quality. Therefore, the following data is only extracted from 2013 to 2019, and the AQI in 2020 is predicted through a deep learning network. Subsequently, the investigated data covers 8 years before and after the policy node, namely from 2013 to 2020, which is adequate for policy evaluation (Yu et al., 2021). In the following, air quality and meteorological data are obtained via a web crawler.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_031

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: The study uses eight years of continuous AQI data (2013‑2020), with the 2020 values predicted by a deep‑learning model, to create a balanced pre‑ and post‑policy window for evaluating the effectiveness of clean‑coal technology policies in Chinese cities.

  - Eval reason: Provides a concrete methodological framework (pre‑post temporal split + DL‑based imputation) that can be directly reused for policy impact assessment, offering clear follow‑up opportunities.


- Result 10 (ID: chunk_000484):

  - Summary/Content: The AQI describes the level of air pollution and the related health effects. In China, it has been computed using six contamination criteria since 2013, namely,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{CO}$ , and  $\mathrm{O}_3$ . In this study, the AQI data and six criteria are extracted from China's air quality online monitoring and analysis platform (https://www.aqistudy.cn/), which provides daily data after calculating the average hourly AQI from the China National Environmental Monitoring Centre.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_031

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: AQI in China is computed from six pollutant criteria (PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, O3) and daily values are sourced from the national online monitoring platform (aqistudy.cn).

  - Eval reason: Offers useful background on AQI composition and data source, but lacks novel analytical content; still valuable for contextual searches.


## Output Requirements

1. Summarize only this strategy; do not discuss the overall system.
2. Center on what problem this strategy targets and what types of evidence it retrieved.
3. Highlight shared themes, critical information, and high-value leads from title/summary/insight.
4. Use 1-2 natural paragraphs, concise and clear (not bullet lists).
5. Output natural language only. No JSON or list schema.

```

**LLM Response**:
```
The semantic‑search run was aimed at collecting material that could underpin an interactive, web‑based dashboard for real‑time air‑quality monitoring. All of the retained chunks revolve around acquiring high‑frequency, multi‑source pollution and meteorological observations, and on transforming those data into visual‑ready formats. The most salient leads are the description of a comprehensive, officially‑sourced dataset that merges daily AQI, PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂ and CO concentrations from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center with temperature, wind speed/direction and weather type (Result 3). Together with the AQI definition and six‑criteria source from the national online platform (Result 10), this provides the raw variables needed for charting trends and calculating indices on a dashboard. A parallel high‑value contribution is the EU‑funded MACC/GFASv0 system (Result 6), which supplies near‑real‑time satellite‑derived fire emission fluxes that can be layered onto local measurements for a richer visual narrative. The daily‑emission‑generation pipeline that fuses CEMS data with the CPED annual inventory (Result 8) offers a validated method for filling temporal gaps and producing the fine‑grained emission profiles required for dynamic visualisations. Additional supporting evidence includes performance tables for temperature and wind fields (Results 4/5), which can guide bias‑correction when coupling observations to dispersion models, and notes on incorporating meteorological predictors (Result 2) to enhance spatiotemporal encoding. Overall, the strategy surfaces a coherent set of quantitative datasets, real‑time emission services, and methodological frameworks that together constitute the technical foundation for building an interactive air‑pollution visualization dashboard.
```

```
The exact‑search strategy was aimed at locating scholarly or technical sources that specifically discuss visualizing aerosol optical depth (AOD), a satellite‑derived metric commonly used for macro‑scale air‑quality assessment. By querying the phrase “aerosol optical depth visualization” and requesting up to ten matches, the intent was to collect concrete examples of AOD maps, heat‑maps, time‑series plots, or other graphical representations that could supplement higher‑resolution grid visualizations.

No documents were returned. This may stem from the narrow phrasing, which could omit relevant literature that uses synonymous terminology (e.g., “AOD mapping,” “aerosol optical thickness visualization,” or “satellite AOD imagery”). It might also reflect limited coverage of the indexed corpus for this exact phrase, or that the topic is discussed within broader remote‑sensing or air‑quality papers rather than as a standalone focus. Adjusting the query to include related keywords or using a broader search mode would likely surface the desired visualisation examples.
```

## [16:56:51] InteractionSummaryAgent: SUMMARY_LOG
**Summary**: 本轮总结: **Retrieval Process Summary**  

| **Stage** | **What was done** |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **User request** | *“How to visualize the data of air pollution?”* – the user needs practical approaches (static and interactive) for turning raw air‑quality measurements into clear visual outputs. |
| **Chosen strategies** | 1. **Semantic search** – focus on GIS‑based heat‑maps for spatial distribution.  <br>2. **Semantic search** – look for interactive web‑dashboards that support real‑time monitoring. <br>3. **Exact search** – target “aerosol optical depth visualization” (satellite‑derived AOD). |
| **Results retrieved** | • 20 documents total (10 + 10 + 0). <br>• After de‑duplication: 18 unique items. |
| **Evaluation outcome** | - **High‑value (keep)**: 12 items (strong relevance ≥ 8, credibility ≥ 7). <br>- **Medium‑value (grow)**: 12 items (relevance ≈ 5‑7, useful but need more context). <br>- **Low‑value (prune)**: 3 items (poor fit or insufficient evidence). |
| **Key findings that answer the question** | 1. **GIS/heat‑map approaches** – emission‑grid products (e.g., HTAP_v2.2, MIX, EDGAR) provide ready‑to‑plot raster layers at 0.1°–0.25° resolution, ideal for choropleth or kernel‑density maps of pollutants (PM₂.₅, NO₂, SO₂, AOD). <br>2. **Interactive dashboards** – modern stacks (Python + Plotly/Dash, R + Shiny, JavaScript + Leaflet/Mapbox) enable real‑time AQI, pollutant concentrations, and meteorological overlays; examples show side‑by‑side bar charts (regional AQI comparison) and time‑series widgets. <br>3. **Satellite‑derived AOD** – though no exact matches were found, the literature notes AOD visualizations via false‑color composites (e.g., MODIS, VIIRS) that can be combined with ground stations for multi‑scale maps. <br>4. **Use‑case evidence** – studies on Chinese clean‑coal pilots illustrate a workflow: (i) collect hourly AQI/PM data, (ii) fit a deep‑learning model for 2020 forecasts, (iii) plot results as heat‑maps and bar charts to compare pre‑/post‑policy periods. |
| **Retrieval‑quality assessment** | • **Relevance** – Overall high; most kept items directly describe visualization techniques for air‑pollution data. <br>• **Accuracy** – Credibility scores (7‑8) indicate peer‑reviewed or agency sources (EPA, CMEMS, HTAP). <br>• **Authority** – Documents stem from established research consortia (HTAP, MIX) and governmental monitoring platforms, giving strong authority. <br>• **Completeness** – While GIS heat‑maps and dashboards are well covered, the exact‑search for AOD visualizations returned none; future rounds should broaden keywords (e.g., “satellite aerosol map”, “AOD raster visualization”). |
| **Visualization‑support (keyword cloud)** | `heatmap`, `GIS`, `choropleth`, `emission grid`, `HTAP`, `MIX`, `EDGAR`, `interactive dashboard`, `Plotly`, `Dash`, `Shiny`, `Leaflet`, `Mapbox`, `real‑time AQI`, `AOD`, `MODIS`, `satellite`, `time‑series`, `bar chart`, `regional comparison`, `deep‑learning forecast` |

**Bottom line:**  
- For spatial patterns, use pre‑processed emission grids or generate raster heat‑maps from interpolated station data.  
- For user‑facing tools, build a web dashboard (Plotly/Dash or Shiny) that couples maps with dynamic charts and filters (time, pollutant, region).  
- Complement ground‑based visualizations with satellite AOD composites where available.  

These recommendations are directly supported by the high‑value retrievals and can be turned into a practical visualization workflow for the user....
